finding: `lock_timeout_seconds` is introduced as a new tunable workflow field, but no agent decision consumes it: the agent neither reads nor sets it (raising it is a user-approved decision per the project's own rules), and the retry-with-backoff behavior — the only part that removes a failed-command retry round-trip — behaves identically against a hardcoded default. Exposing it as a knob adds a field to `tp config --resolved`, the docs, and the workflow surface without feeding any agent branch, in direct tension with the spec's own §2.6 ('no new knobs unless a knob removes a round-trip').
suggestion: Keep the retry/backoff behavior but hold the timeout as an internal constant at the default value; if it must be retained as a field for parity with `gate_timeout_seconds`, state that parity exception explicitly so §2.6 is not silently violated.

finding: The bookkeeping entry's `ref` is fully derivable for the `config` kind (ref = path basename, byte-identical to data already in the sibling `path` field) and near-derivable for the `round` kind (the round number is the numeral inside `path`). Only the `closure` kind carries genuinely new information (the task id, which the shared `<base>.tasks.json` path does not contain). Emitting `ref` uniformly adds a JSON key per entry whose value the agent can reproduce at zero cost for two of three kinds — pure overhead on the very surface (hc-mode resume) that exists to minimize agent bytes.
suggestion: Emit `ref` only for the `closure` kind; for `config` drop it (path basename is already in `path`) and for `round` drop it unless the numeral is not trivially parseable from the filename.

finding: §5.2 emits a `closure` bookkeeping entry "per task whose managed fields (`status`, `closed_at`, `gate_passed_at`, `commit_sha`) changed since HEAD". tp's managed-fields invariant is the larger set (status, started_at, closed_at, closed_reason, gate_passed_at, gate_skipped_reason, commit_sha/commit_shas); the parenthetical labels a 4-element subset as "managed fields" and notably omits `closed_reason` (unambiguously written at every close) and the canonical `commit_shas`. It is not a detection gap in practice — `status` and `closed_at` co-change on every close/reopen, so the subset still fires — but the clause reuses the defined term "managed fields" for a partial list, so an implementer cannot tell whether to diff the full managed set or only the four named fields.
suggestion: Rename the parenthetical to signal an intentional subset (e.g. "whose closure-marking fields (`status`, `closed_at`, …) changed") or list the full managed-fields set and state the entry fires when any of them changed since HEAD.

finding: §9.1 defines four skip reasons each with its own trigger, but the triggers can co-occur on a single role with no stated precedence: (a) a role with empty `focus` AND a non-matching domain satisfies both `no-checklist-items` and `domain-mismatch`; (b) a role with empty `focus` under `--diff-from` satisfies both `no-checklist-items` and `no-spec-change` (an empty focus is trivially 'outside the changed sections'). The spec says reason is 'one of' the four but never says which wins when several match, so two testers constructing such a role would assert different `reason` values for identical input.
suggestion: Pin an evaluation order, e.g. 'reasons are evaluated in this order — domain-mismatch, no-checklist-items, no-spec-change, no-baseline — and the first match is reported' so the emitted reason is deterministic when conditions overlap.
